observations.recommendation:
1.23 We recommended that Management direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant (MA) to (i) accrue the cost of construction of all ongoing
public infrastructure projects at year-end to the appropriate CIP account, based on
the percentage of completion as reported by the MEO; (ii) draw a journal entry
voucher (JEV) to adjust the related Accumulated Depreciation accounts to correct the
noted understatement in the provision for depreciation expense amounting to
₱377,446.97, if warranted; and (iii) recompute the Accumulated Depreciation of other
PPE accounts, considering the actual delivery or completion dates, and make the
necessary adjustments thereof; and
b) Municipal Treasurer (MT), and/or General Services Officer (GSO) to (i)
prepare and maintain PCs for each type of PPE; (ii) through the Inventory
Committee, investigate and reconcile the noted discrepancies between the accounting
records and the result of physical inventory; (iii) ensure the registration of all motor
vehicles with the LTO to prevent the incurrence of unnecessary penalties and fines;
and (iv) secure insurance coverage of all insurable properties with the GSIS to
mitigate risks in the event of damage to, or loss of, properties due to fire, earthquake,
storm, or other catastrophes.

2.13 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
(a) MA to (i) ensure that collections are recorded under the CIB account only upon
confirmation of actual deposit; and (ii) accurately record all collections in the
appropriate accounting period corresponding to the date of receipt; and
(b) MT to promptly deposit all collections intact, either on the day of receipt or no
later than the next banking day, thereby properly safeguarding government funds
from risks of theft, loss, misapplication, and/or misappropriation.

3.24 We recommended that Management direct the:
a. MA to (i) ensure compliance with Section 20, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for
LGUs in the proper recognition and setting up of RPT/SET Receivables; and (ii)
reconcile the RPT/SET Receivable accounts with the MT’s Certified List of
Delinquent Real Property Taxpayers, and make the necessary adjustments, if any,
to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements;
b. Municipal Assessor, MT and MA to reconcile all RPT-related reports, such as
the Assessment Roll, Certified List of Delinquent Real Property Taxpayers, and the
Abstract of RPTs. This is to identify and address any discrepancies in a timely manner
and to uphold the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of RPT-related records and
reports;
c. Municipal Assessor to ensure that all FAAS are properly accomplished, and
duly signed. Additionally, coordinate with the PAO to secure copies of all FAAS for
properties assessed at the PAO level. A well-organized, complete, and readily
available filing system must be maintained to support accurate assessments and to
facilitate future audits; and
d. MA (i) conduct a comprehensive assessment of the collectability of its RPT
receivables, taking into account key factors such as the aging of accounts, historical
collection rates, and the economic conditions of the locality; (ii) establish an
appropriate allowance for impairment to accurately reflect the estimated
uncollectible portion of RPT receivables; (iii) implement policies and procedures to
regularly review and adjust the impairment allowance as necessary; and (iv) make
the appropriate adjusting journal entries to correct the financial records.

4.14 We recommended that the LCE:
a) instruct the Inventory Committee to:
i. perform a periodic physical inventory count of supplies and materials; and
ii. prepare the corresponding RPCI for submission to the AT not later than July
31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
b) require the GSO to:
i. maintain a complete and updated record of stock cards for each inventory
item for reconciliation with the ledger cards of the MAO;
ii. closely coordinate with the MAO for the conduct of thorough verification and
reconciliation of the RPCI and accounting records to identify and resolve the
nature and causes of any possible variance, holding the designated custodians
accountable and/or responsible for the discrepancies; and
c) enjoin the foregoing concerned Offices to adopt written internal policies and
guidelines relative to accounting, reporting, and monitoring of supplies and materials.

5.24 We recommended that Management direct the:
a. HRMO Head to (i) initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of
balances in the employee leave cards; (ii) revisit its process of maintaining and
reporting employee leave records, which should include updating and encoding of
employee leave cards regularly, generating quarterly reports for timely submission to
the MAO and other concerned offices, and other internal control measures to provide
reliable information; and (iii) certify the copy of employee leave cards when
processing claims of separated employees; and
b. MA to (i) draw a JEV to record the initial set-up of provision for earned leave
credits in the books of accounts of the ensuing year as recommended below:
Account Name Dr. Cr.
Prior Period Adjustment ₱27,315,348.55
Leave Benefits Payable ₱27,315,348.55
(ii) recognize subsequent provisions at least quarterly; (iii) be guided with the
suggested journal entries in accounting for transactions related to leave benefits as
illustrated in Annex C.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2024-009; and (iv) prepare and certify
the computation of TLB for separated employees to ensure accuracy and compliance.

6.11 We recommended that Management (a) direct the MA to (1) recognize
expenses when incurred, in accordance with IPSAS I; and (2) ensure that all JEVs are
properly supported with the necessary documentation, as required under Section 111
of P.D. No. 1445; and (b) require the GSO to conduct, prepare, and submit a year-end
physical inventory of supplies and goods for distribution to the MAO, in compliance
with Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs.

7.6 We recommended that Management:
a. ensure all financial assistance programs or disbursements are supported by
either a Sangguniang Resolution or formally approved internal guidelines; and
b. develop and implement clear, objective, and standardized criteria, especially
for assistance involving varying amounts to be incorporated in the authorizing
resolution or internal guidelines, and applied consistently to all beneficiaries.

Nonetheless, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant (MA) records the CA for CF under the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer (SDO) account instead of Due from Officers and Employees account to avoid misstatement of Receivable accounts.

Designated GSO to (i) adhere with the proper procedure in the acceptance, inspection
and issuance of PPE; and (ii) in coordination with the IC to comply with the
requirements on the disposal of unserviceable properties; (b) MA to (i) recognize

account; (c) MT/PO and the Designated GSO to (i) prepare and maintain PCs for each

₱35,113,610.94; and (iii) instruct the IC to coordinate with them in strategizing the
conduct of complete physical inventory in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-
006; and (d) MT/PO and the Designated GSO to submit LTO Registration of motor
vehicles for verification and ensure that all vehicles are registered regularly to avoid
the incurrence of unnecessary penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the (a) MA to (i) reconcile
the discrepancies noted in the fund transfer with the concerned government agencies;
(ii) trace and confirm with the concerned government agency the records/documents
relative to the long outstanding and unsubstantiated balances; and (iii) file a request
for authority to write-off the dormant fund transfers from TESDA with COA after
the conditions and requirements pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008 have been
fully satisfied; and (b) MT to ensure that the reporting requirements outlined in the
MOA for the SFP will be strictly observed.

observations.recommendation:
1.4	We recommended that Management: (a) direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to: (i) adopt public bidding as the primary mode of procurement; and (ii) adhere strictly to the rules and regulations of R.A. No. 9184, especially in implementing an alternative mode of procurements for emergency purchases; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) coordinate with the BAC for the completion and submission of the documentary requirements and ensure that only DVs with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment; and (ii) submit the DVs for COVID-19 related transactions with complete supporting documents for prompt verification and timely communication of any noted deficiencies.
1.5	The Management committed to comply with the completion of documentary requirements for the disbursement of COVID-19-related purchases and observed the threshold limitation for alternative modes of procurement under R.A. No. 9184.
Likewise, all unsubmitted DVs, will be forwarded to the Audit Team the soonest possible.

2.9	We recommended that Management instruct the MHO and staff, in coordination with the Human Resources Management Officer and Municipal Accountant, to submit (i) the List of Eligible Frontliners duly approved by the Local Health Board; (ii) DTRs; (iii) duties and responsibilities with Accomplishment Reports of regular, casuals and JO personnel; and (iv) Appointments/Plantilla for casuals, JOs and BHWs for us to evaluate the validity and propriety of the grant and disbursement of the AHDP and SRA.
2.10	During the exit conference, Management committed to provide list of eligible BHWs together with the complete requirements as recommended by the Audit Team.

3.4	We recommended that Management undertake the following corrective measures:
file a request for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers with COA after the conditions and requirements pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 have been fully satisfied; and
trace the records/documents relative to the unreturned balance of all completed projects and coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the return of unused funds to the SAs or to the National Treasury, as the case may be.
3.5	Management will send confirmation letters to SAs and will return unutilized funds once validated, while request for write-off will be made once dormant funds are verified.

5.6	We recommended that Management direct the:
LDRRMC/MDRRMO to monitor and evaluate the use of the Fund and strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objectives of the fund and the desired benefits; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) adjust the TL – DRRMF and Due to NGAs account; and (ii) submit the monthly Utilization Report within the reglementary period.
5.7	Management committed to comply with the monthly submission of Utilization Report.

6.3   We recommended that Management:
require the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain CIPLC and coordinate closely with the Municipal Engineer’s Office to monitor project status and facilitate proper accounting for CIP; (ii) recognize expenditures for asphalting of roads as Repairs and Maintenance Expense instead of capitalizing the cost to Road Networks account in conformity with COA Circular No. 2015-008; and (iii) draw JEVs for the recommended adjustments to record correction on the (1) erroneous capitalization of ongoing construction projects; (2) erroneous capitalization for asphalting of roads, and (3) valuation of the Land account and unrecognized parcels of land; if warranted;
secure proofs of ownership for untitled lands claimed by the Municipality to avoid future third-party claims; and
instruct the Municipal Treasurer or General Services Officer to provide adequate insurance on all its insurable properties to safeguard the same from possible loss due to unforeseen events.
6.4	During the exit conference, Management recognizes the importance of securing the title of ownership over the land properties. Municipal Assessor already initiated steps in securing land titles such as surveying and relocation of lot boundaries. It has been assured that succeeding steps will be done to process the issuance of land titles.
6.5	Municipal Accountant committed to effect necessary adjusting entries to correct the erroneous capitalizations to PPE accounts and submit JEVs pertaining thereto to the office of the Auditor. Moreover, Municipal Engineer agreed to the Auditor’s recommendation to recognize expenditures for road asphalting as Repairs and Maintenance Expenses.
6.6	Management also discussed the reason for non-insurance of PPEs was due to limited budget. Nonetheless, they committed to allot adequate funds for the acquisition of insurance for its insurable properties.

7.6	We recommended that Management:
coordinate with the DENR Officials in locating and acquiring a suitable lot for the construction of SLF and seek financial assistance from the national government or private organization for possible funding for its construction;
send personnel assigned in the operation of MRF to training, seminars and let them observe best practices in other LGUs like brick making, hollow blocks and other products out of crushed plastics; and
intensify the information campaign including strict implementation on prohibited acts particularly on open burning among its constituents, thereby promoting a smoke-free and healthy environment.
7.7	During the exit conference, Management disclosed that they cannot locate a suitable area for SLF due to a limited budget and the Municipality is observing great caution not to contaminate the rivers and the water supply.
7.8	On the other hand, training were already scheduled for MRF personnel to utilize its use. Management also plans to include household members to participate in MRF operations. They were also looking into projects being offered that will provide additional machines that will further improve the MRF operation.
7.9	Moreover, Management is already taking measures to intensify the campaign against open burning at the barangay level. Flyers are being distributed to disseminate information and bring awareness about the effects of burning to promote a healthy environment.

8.3	We recommended that Management (a) issue an EO to strengthen the GFPS by reorganizing the GFPS Execom in accordance with the required composition of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; (b) exert additional efforts in the implementation of all planned PPAs; and (c) direct the LFC to properly include DILG endorsed GAD PPAs in the LGU’s AIP and Annual Budget to facilitate implementation.
8.4	Management admitted during the exit conference that there were lapses in the implementation of PPAs due to a lack of proper knowledge about GAD, thus, they requested PWC to conduct training with their concerned Municipal Officials.

9.4	We recommended that Management take the following courses of actions:
Municipal Treasurer to (i) demand payment of monthly rentals that have remained uncollected as at year-end; (ii) collect security deposits based on the contract and refund security deposits of former stallholders or use for the restoration of the leased premises; and (iii) provide a copy of lease contract together with other pertinent documents to support the recording of rental receivables by the MAO;
Market Committee to (i) institute the civil remedies available pursuant to Section 174 of R.A. No. 7160; and (ii) enforce the cancellation of the lease contract/agreement of delinquent lessees and the revocation of their right to occupy the stalls;
BPLO to (i) execute a contract of lease with each stallholder signed by both parties for the full protection and interest of the LGU; and (ii) require market stallholders to apply and annually renew Business/Mayor’s Permit; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) adjust the security deposit of ₱20,000.00 from Other Deferred Credits Account to Customers' Deposits Payable Account; and (ii) accrue revenues that remained uncollected as at year-end.
9.5	Management disclosed that late payments were due to the pandemic’s financial and economic effects, wherein the extension of payments was provided as consideration to stallholders. Nevertheless, cancellation of contracts and revocation of rights will be implemented after the consummation of the provided grace period. Likewise, notarized contracts of stallholders were already released and they continue to expedite other contracts to be notarized. Moreover, the Business/Mayor’s Permit is still in process and awaiting approval from different offices.
9.6	Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the Auditor’s recommendation on accrual of revenues at year-end, while the Municipal Treasurer collected security deposits from new stallholders.

10.3	We recommended and Management agreed to direct:
BAC Members and TWG to (i) ensure that prospective bidders submit complete and correct eligibility requirements before they are marked as “passed” and declared as Lowest Calculated and Responsive Bid (LCRB) during post-qualification; and (ii) prepare Invitation to Observers for Pre-Bid Conference or any procurement activity at least five days before its schedule;
Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the BAC members, to submit the foregoing lacking documents including a relative contract and other documents forming part thereof, within five CDs for awarded contracts to substantiate the disbursements made and ensure that all payments are supported with complete documentation; and
Municipal Engineer/Implementing Unit to use the formats and computation for POW and DUPA as prescribed under DPWH DO Nos. 163, series of 2015, and 197, series of 2016.

11.5	We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately transmit the unsubmitted accounts covering the period September to December 2021 and adhere strictly to the prescribed timeframes on the submission of report and preparation/submittal of financial reports to the Audit Team.

12.6	We recommended and Management agreed to advise the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to adhere faithfully to RMC No. 23-2012 on the prompt remittance of taxes to avoid the incurrence of interests and surcharges.

13.7	We recommended and Management agreed to advise the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to adhere faithfully to the payment schedule of premium contributions withheld on its employees in accordance with PhilHealth Circular No. 001 series of 2014.

14.6	We recommended and Management agreed to advise the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to adhere faithfully to the payment schedule of premium contributions withheld on its employees in accordance with R.A. No. 9679.

15.8	We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to (a) continually exert effort to comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 8291 and its IRR  on the withholding and remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations of employees; (b) ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in full; and (c) perform the necessary reconciliation with the GSIS to trace the undocumented balance of ₱87,108.34 for proper disposition.
Management informed the Audit Team during the exit conference that differences were due to step increments, newly hired employees, and salary adjustments that were not updated in the GSIS portal. Ongoing tracing is being done to reconcile the difference.

We recommended that Management advise the Municipal Accountant to locate or retrieve the pertinent documents to substantiate the unaccounted balances and take necessary action to establish the accuracy of PPE balances or consider requesting an authority from COA to write off unidentified PPEs, provided proper justifications are supported to the request.

Municipal Engineer to (i) submit written justification for the immeasurable delays on 11 projects and any Suspension/Extension Order issued on these projects; (ii) ensure that all items in the Program of Works/Scope of Works are strictly followed; (iii) ensure that contractors provide safety gears to construction workers; (iv) regularly monitor infrastructure projects to avoid delays in the completion of the project; and (v) coordinate with the contractors to comply with the required posting of information and publicity on PPAs of government agencies and to inform the Audit Team within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the start of the program/project/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the project site.

direct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within the set schedule thru close monitoring; and

Members of the Sangguniang Bayan consider (i) enacting appropriate ordinance on the regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyances to prevent undesirable outcomes as a result of unregulated proliferation of tricycles along the national highways, which may pose hazards and imminent danger to pedestrians and motorists’ lives and properties; (ii) recommend alternative routes that would be accessible and conducive to  commuters and tricycle operators; and (iii) impose sanctions against illegal parking; and
Local Chief Executive instruct the traffic enforcers to diligently perform their duties by strictly monitoring the condition of the parking areas  within the right-of-way of national roads, particularly in front of the Castillejos Public Market to comply with the DPWH Department Order No. 73, and deploy more traffic enforcers during market days and busy hours.

We recommended that the Property Officer (a) conduct actual inventory of all installed CCTVs and identify defective units for withdrawal and evaluation as to necessity of repair or replacement by the supplier and evaluate the need to adjust the angle of CCTV installation at the lamp post for better visibility; (b) request for the technical assistance of the Engineering Office personnel and CCTV Monitoring Unit, for  further evaluation of the technical specifications of the CCTVs  in order to maximize the benefits of their utilization, taking into consideration the cost effectiveness, performance efficiency and effectiveness of the CCTV; and (c) submit an explanation for not taking an action against the supplier for the unauthorized replacement of some units.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the: (a) implementing departments of the Municipality to (i) submit explanation letter on the late delivery of the school furniture and claim appropriate liquidated damages for the late delivery; and (ii) submit immediately to the Accounting Unit all the necessary documents to support liquidation reports to the National Government Agencies concerned so that the succeeding release of BuB will not be delayed; and (b)  Local Poverty Reduction and Action Team (LPRAT) to closely monitor the  projects that were identified and programmed under the Bottom-up Budgeting for their implementation within the set schedule and time frame.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:

(a) submit a Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card, Certified List of Taxpayers with account balances and the complete aging schedule of receivables as of December 31, 2015 in accordance with Section 20, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units;

(b) ensure continuous/proper monitoring of receivables by sending demand letters to taxpayers on their delinquent accounts and intensify efforts on the collection drive for an increased RPT and SEF collection efficiency; and

(c) avail of all legal and administrative remedies either by administrative action through levy on real property or judicial action.
